content:
# Judges Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_269.jpg, JDG_page_270.jpg

### Othniel, Ehud, and Shamgar

1. These are the nations the LORD left in the land to test those Israelites who had not experienced the wars of Canaan.

2. He did this to teach warfare to the new generations of Israelites who had not had that experience before.

3. These are the nations: the five rulers of the Philistines, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites living in the Lebanon mountains from Mount Baal-hermon to Lebo-hamath.

4. These nations were left to test the Israelites — to see whether they would obey the commands the LORD had given their ancestors through Moses.

5. So the Israelites lived among the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.

6. And the Israelites intermarried with them. Israelite sons married their daughters, and Israelite daughters were given in marriage to their sons. And the Israelites served their gods.

7. The Israelites did evil in the LORD's sight. They forgot the LORD their God and served the images of Baal and the Asherah poles.

8. Then the LORD burned with anger against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of King Cushan-rishathaim of Aram-naharaim. And the Israelites served Cushan-rishathaim for eight years.

9. But when the people of Israel cried out to the LORD for help, the LORD raised up a rescuer to save them. His name was Othniel, the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother.

10. The Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he became Israel's judge. He went to war, and the LORD gave him victory over King Cushan-rishathaim of Aram. So Othniel overpowered him.

11. And there was peace in the land for forty years. Then Othniel son of Kenaz died.

12. Once again the Israelites did evil in the LORD's sight, and the LORD gave King Eglon of Moab power over Israel because of their evil.

13. Eglon enlisted the Ammonites and Amalekites as allies, and together they attacked and defeated Israel, taking possession of Jericho, the city of palms.

14. And the Israelites served Eglon of Moab for eighteen years.

15. But when the people of Israel cried out to the LORD for help, the LORD again raised up a rescuer to save them. His name was Ehud son of Gera, a left-handed man of the tribe of Benjamin. The Israelites sent him to deliver their tribute money to King Eglon of Moab.

16. So Ehud made a double-edged dagger that was about a foot and a half long, and he strapped it to his right thigh under his clothing.

17. He brought the tribute to King Eglon of Moab, who was very fat.

18. After delivering the tribute, Ehud sent home those who had carried it.

19. But when Ehud reached the stone idols near Gilgal, he turned back and said to the king, "I have a secret message for you." So the king commanded his servants, "Be quiet!" and they all left the room.

20. Ehud walked over to Eglon as he was sitting alone in a cool upstairs room. And Ehud said, "I have a message from God for you!" As King Eglon rose from his seat,

21. Ehud reached with his left hand, pulled out the dagger strapped to his right thigh, and plunged it into the king's belly.

22. The dagger went so deep that the handle disappeared beneath the king's fat. So Ehud did not pull out the dagger, and the king's bowels emptied.

23. Then Ehud closed and locked the doors of the room behind him and escaped through the porch.

24. After Ehud was gone, the king's servants returned. When they found the doors locked, they thought he might be using the latrine in the room.

25. So they waited, but when the king didn't come out after a long and embarrassing delay, they finally took a key and opened the doors. And there was their master — Loss dead on the floor!

26. While the servants waited, Ehud escaped. He passed the stone idols and made his way to Seirah.

27. When he arrived in the hill country of Ephraim, he sounded a call to arms. Then the Israelites came down from the hills with Ehud leading them.

28. "Follow me," he said, "for the LORD has given you victory over Moab your enemy!" So they followed him down to the Jordan River, where they seized the shallow crossings.

29. They killed about ten thousand Moabites that day — all strong and able warriors. Not one escaped.

30. So Moab was conquered by Israel that day, and there was peace in the land for eighty years.

31. After Ehud, Shamgar son of Anath rescued Israel. He once killed six hundred Philistines with an ox goad.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The Ehud story (vv.15-30):** This is one of the most dramatic stories in the Bible — and intentionally funny. The narrator takes obvious delight in the details: the fat king, the hidden dagger, the locked door, the embarrassed servants waiting outside while their king lies dead. The story celebrates the cleverness of God's unlikely deliverer — a left-handed Benjaminite (ironic, since "Benjamin" means "son of the right hand").

**Verse 31 — Shamgar:** One of the briefest judge accounts — a single verse. An ox goad was a long, pointed farming tool, about eight feet long. Shamgar's use of an improvised weapon against 600 Philistines echoes the theme of Judges: God uses the unexpected to deliver.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
